name: writing-lib-functions
description: Use this skill when you need to write lib functions in `srs/lib` for the Next.js app
capabilities: Writes library functions in the srs/lib directory for Next.js applications
Enforces modular code structure with file size limits
Separates concerns into multiple focused files
Follows project conventions for code organization
author: Emz1998
securitySummary: Static analysis flagged backticks in markdown and YAML as shell execution patterns. This is a false positive. The skill contains only documentation with inline code formatting. No executable code, network access, or sensitive operations. Safe for publication.
valueStatement: Developers working on Next.js applications need consistent, maintainable library functions. This skill provides clear guidelines for writing modular, well-organized code in the srs/lib directory.
userTitle: Write modular lib functions for Next.js
limitations: Does not generate tests or documentation for the functions
Does not validate function logic or edge cases
Does not integrate with external APIs or databases
Does not provide code examples or templates

useCases.title: Create utility functions
useCases.description: Add new helper functions to handle common tasks like data formatting, validation, or string manipulation in the Next.js application.

useCases.title: Refactor existing code
useCases.description: Split large utility files into smaller, focused modules following the 50-line limit per file guideline.

useCases.title: Add data layer functions
useCases.description: Create new functions for database operations, API integrations, or data transformation logic within the srs/lib directory.

promptTemplates.title: Basic function request
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a lib function in srs/lib that formats dates to a human-readable format.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need to add a simple utility function

promptTemplates.title: Validation function
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a validation function in srs/lib that checks if an email address is valid.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need form validation logic

promptTemplates.title: Data transformation
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a function in srs/lib that transforms API response data into the format needed by the frontend components.
promptTemplates.scenario: When working with external API data

promptTemplates.title: Complex utility with refactoring
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to add a new utility for parsing and validating user input. Write it as a modular function in srs/lib. Keep each file under 50 lines and separate concerns into multiple files if needed.
promptTemplates.scenario: When creating a complex utility that needs to be well-organized

bestPractices: Keep each file under 50 lines of code to improve readability and maintainability
Separate related functions into their own files by responsibility
Use descriptive file names that indicate the purpose of the contained functions
Group related utilities in subdirectories when the lib grows large
antiPatterns: Putting unrelated utility functions in a single catch-all file like utils.ts
Creating files with hundreds of lines of code that mix different concerns
Duplicating similar logic across multiple files instead of extracting shared helpers
Adding business logic or component-specific code to the lib directory

faq.question: Where should I place new library functions?
faq.answer: Create new files in srs/lib or its subdirectories. Use descriptive names like formatters/, validators/, or helpers/ to organize by purpose.

faq.question: What is the recommended file size limit?
faq.answer: Keep each file under 50 lines of code. If a file grows larger, refactor it into smaller, focused modules.

faq.question: Can I create subdirectories in srs/lib?
faq.answer: Yes. Create subdirectories like srs/lib/utils/, srs/lib/validators/, or srs/lib/formatters/ to group related functions.

faq.question: Should I add TypeScript types in lib files?
faq.answer: Yes. Include proper TypeScript interfaces and type definitions within each file to maintain type safety.

faq.question: How should I name library function files?
faq.answer: Use kebab-case for file names. Group similar functions together. For example, use date-formatters.ts instead of dateFormatter.ts.

faq.question: What goes in srs/lib versus other directories?
faq.answer: srs/lib should contain pure utility functions, formatters, validators, and helpers. Keep business logic, components, and hooks in their respective directories.
